item.description:
Atmospheric Stability

Formal definition

An AtmosphericStability triple (A,t,ℓ)(A, t, \ell):

(A∈At,  t∈T,  ℓ∈Locale)(A \in \mathcal{A}_t,\; t \in T,\; \ell \in \mathrm{Locale})

where:

  • At\mathcal{A}_t is the atmospheric column fiber at history tt — the complete Heyting algebra of column state propositions at tt; elements of At\mathcal{A}_t are statements about the vertical temperature profile (the sounding), the dewpoint profile, the wind profile, the potential temperature θ\theta and equivalent potential temperature θe\theta_e at each level, and the derived stability indices (CAPE, CIN, LFC height) of the atmospheric column at locale ℓ\ell at history tt
  • t∈Tt \in T is the analysis history — the position in the history monoid; each step s∈Σs \in \Sigma advances the column through one forcing cycle: one episode of surface heating, one advection of warm/cold air, one convective initiation event; the onset of convection at the LFC and the triggering of a deep cumulonimbus are specific step atoms in Σ\Sigma
  • ℓ\ell is the locale — the geographical column being analyzed; the locale’s surface type, land-sea boundary, orography, and proximity to moisture sources determine the surface fluxes that build the thermodynamic history

The stability nuclear pair. The fiber HtH_t at history tt carries the two canonical nuclei. Let Σ\Sigma be the step monoid for the column site (T,J)(T, J). Each step s∈Σs \in \Sigma represents one atmospheric perturbation — one vertical displacement event: a gust, a turbulent eddy, an orographic push, a frontal lifting pulse. The commutation condition s⊥ts \perp t means displacement ss is genuinely independent of the current column history tt: a small vertical displacement in a cap-free atmosphere acts regardless of what the prior temperature history was.

Greenhouse Effect

Formal definition

A GreenhouseRadiation triple (F,t,ℓ)(F, t, \ell):

(F∈Ft,  t∈T,  ℓ∈Locale)(F \in \mathcal{F}_t,\; t \in T,\; \ell \in \mathrm{Locale})

where:

  • Ft\mathcal{F}_t is the radiative flux fiber at optical-depth history tt — the complete Heyting algebra of radiative flux state propositions at tt; elements of Ft\mathcal{F}_t are statements about the spectral irradiance field I(ν,z)I(\nu, z) at wavelength ν\nu and altitude zz, the longwave and shortwave irradiance profiles F↓(z)F_{\downarrow}(z) and F↑(z)F_{\uparrow}(z), the net flux Fnet(z)F_\mathrm{net}(z), and the derived radiative quantities (optical depth profile τ(ν,z)\tau(\nu, z), brightness temperature Tb(ν)T_b(\nu), outgoing longwave radiation at the top of atmosphere) at locale ℓ\ell at history tt
  • t∈Tt \in T is the optical-depth history — the position in the history monoid; each step s∈Σs \in \Sigma advances the column through one absorbing layer (one pressure-altitude increment from the top of atmosphere downward); the composition s⋆ts \star t adds one more layer of gas below the current top-of-column level; the independence structure s⊥ts \perp t captures the fact that different spectral wavelength bands absorb independently of each other in the first approximation — adding optical depth in the CO2_2 15 μ\mum band does not change the optical depth in the H2_2O 6.3 μ\mum band or the atmospheric window
  • ℓ\ell is the locale — the geographical column; the locale’s surface type, temperature, and emissivity determine the boundary condition at the surface (the lower-boundary constraint on the optical-depth history)

The greenhouse nuclear pair. The fiber Ft\mathcal{F}_t at optical-depth history tt carries the two canonical nuclei. Let Σ\Sigma be the step monoid for the column site (T,J)(T, J). Each step s∈Σs \in \Sigma represents one spectral absorption event at wavelength νs\nu_s: a unit increment of optical depth in one spectral band. The commutation condition s⊥ts \perp t means the absorption event at wavelength νs\nu_s is genuinely independent of the current optical-depth history tt — absorbing in one spectral channel does not alter the absorption state in any other channel (the independent-band approximation that underlies radiative transfer in the atmosphere).

Precipitation

Formal definition

A Precipitation triple (P,t,ℓ)(P, t, \ell):

(P∈Pt,  t∈T,  ℓ∈Locale)(P \in \mathcal{P}_t,\; t \in T,\; \ell \in \mathrm{Locale})

where:

  • Pt\mathcal{P}_t is the atmospheric column fiber at history tt — the complete Heyting algebra of precipitation state propositions at tt; elements of Pt\mathcal{P}_t are statements about the column precipitable water, temperature lapse rate, dewpoint profile, cloud liquid water content, ice water content, hydrometeor size distribution, and surface precipitation rate at locale ℓ\ell at history tt; the fiber inherits its Heyting algebra structure from HtH_t
  • t∈Tt \in T is the analysis history — the position in the history monoid at which the column state is evaluated; each step s∈Σs \in \Sigma advances the column through one moisture cycle: one evaporation event at the surface, one condensation event aloft, one collision-coalescence event between droplets, one updraft or downdraft pulse; the formation of the first precipitation-sized hydrometeor (diameter >0.2 mm> 0.2\,\mathrm{mm}) and the surface arrival of the first raindrop are specific step atoms in Σ\Sigma
  • ℓ\ell is the locale — the spatial domain, from surface to tropopause, over which the column moisture budget is evaluated; the locale determines the boundary conditions: surface moisture flux (evaporation, transpiration), surface temperature, orographic lifting, and advection of moisture from upstream columns

The precipitation nuclear pair. The fiber HtH_t at history tt carries the two canonical nuclei, each corresponding to one of the two independent conditions for surface precipitation. Let Σ\Sigma be the step monoid for the column site (T,J)(T, J). Each step s∈Σs \in \Sigma represents one atmospheric perturbation event — one turbulent mixing episode, one evaporation pulse in the sub-cloud layer, one wind shear event. The commutation condition s⊥ts \perp t means perturbation ss is genuinely independent of the current column history tt: a turbulent mixing event in the sub-cloud layer is independent of the history of moisture accumulation aloft (the mixing occurs regardless of whether there is a cloud overhead). The one-step extension is,t:t→s⋆ti_{s,t} : t \to s \star t is the history extended by one perturbation event; the restriction map H(is,t):Ps⋆t→PtH(i_{s,t}) : \mathcal{P}_{s \star t} \to \mathcal{P}_t sends a post-perturbation column state back to its pre-perturbation state.

Rain

Formal definition

Fix a history tt and the fiber HtH_t. Among the propositions of HtH_t is the precipitation proposition rtr_t. The free KK-module V~t=KHt\widetilde{V}_t = K^{H_t} carries the defect operator δt\delta_t, defined by RelationalHistoryFiberModeSpectralDecompositionDefectOperatorEigenspaces to be diagonal on the basis {ea:a∈Ht}\{e_a : a \in H_t\} with eigenvalue

λa:=∣[a, πt(a)]∣−1\lambda_a := |[a,\, \pi_t(a)]| - 1

where [a,πt(a)][a, \pi_t(a)] is the interval from aa to its joint nuclear image in HtH_t (RelationalHistoryProjectionGapSize), and πt=σt∘Δt\pi_t = \sigma_t \circ \Delta_t is the joint nuclear retraction.

Rain at tt is the condition λrt=0\lambda_{r_t} = 0 — the precipitation proposition is in the zero-eigenspace of δt\delta_t. This is equivalent to rt∈Ht∗r_t \in H_t^*: the projection gap is trivial, meaning πt(rt)=rt\pi_t(r_t) = r_t, meaning neither the saturation nucleus nor the transfer nucleus would move it.

Surface Energy Balance

Formal definition

A SurfaceEnergyBalance triple (E,t,ℓ)(E, t, \ell):

(E∈Et,  t∈T,  ℓ∈Locale)(E \in \mathcal{E}_t,\; t \in T,\; \ell \in \mathrm{Locale})

where:

  • Et\mathcal{E}_t is the surface fiber at history tt — the complete Heyting algebra of surface energy state propositions at tt; elements of Et\mathcal{E}_t are statements about the net radiation (RnR_n), sensible heat flux (HH), latent heat flux (LE\mathrm{LE}), ground heat flux (GG), surface temperature (TsT_s), vapor pressure deficit (es−eae_s - e_a), and aerodynamic and surface resistance (rar_a, rsr_s) at locale ℓ\ell at history tt
  • t∈Tt \in T is the analysis history — the position in the history monoid; each step s∈Σs \in \Sigma advances the surface through one energy exchange cycle: one solar radiation pulse, one turbulent heat flux episode, one evaporation event; sunrise and sunset are specific step atoms in Σ\Sigma that change the topology of the radiation forcing
  • ℓ\ell is the locale — the specific surface patch being analyzed: ocean, forest, bare soil, urban asphalt, ice sheet; the locale’s albedo α\alpha, emissivity ε\varepsilon, surface resistance rsr_s (stomatal control in vegetation), and roughness length z0z_0 are fixed parameters that shape the nuclear pair

The surface energy balance equation at any surface, treating the surface layer as infinitesimally thin (no energy storage):

Tropical Cyclone

Formal definition

A TropicalCyclone triple (S,t,ℓ)(S, t, \ell):

(S∈Tt,  t∈T,  ℓ∈Locale)(S \in \mathcal{T}_t,\; t \in T,\; \ell \in \mathrm{Locale})

where:

  • Tt\mathcal{T}_t is the storm fiber at history tt — the complete Heyting algebra of tropical cyclone state propositions at tt; elements of Tt\mathcal{T}_t are statements about the maximum sustained wind speed, minimum central pressure, eye diameter, sea-surface temperature (SST) profile, ocean heat content, environmental wind shear, and inner-core convective organization of the storm at oceanic locale ℓ\ell at history tt; the fiber inherits its Heyting algebra structure from HtH_t
  • t∈Tt \in T is the analysis history — the position in the history monoid at which the storm state is evaluated; each step s∈Σs \in \Sigma advances the storm through one forcing cycle: one SST-exposure event, one wind-shear pulse, one convective burst in the inner core; the datable intensity thresholds (tropical depression, tropical storm, hurricane, major hurricane) are coarse-grained products of many such steps
  • ℓ\ell is the locale — the oceanic and atmospheric domain over which the storm tracks; the locale determines the SST, ocean heat content, ambient wind shear, tropopause temperature, and mid-level moisture that constitute the environmental boundary conditions

The tropical cyclone nuclear pair. The fiber HtH_t at history tt carries the two canonical nuclei, each corresponding to one of the two independent structural requirements for a sustained tropical cyclone. Let Σ\Sigma be the step monoid for the storm site (T,J)(T, J). Each step s∈Σs \in \Sigma represents one environmental interaction event — one ocean-surface heat flux episode, one wind-shear pulse, one dry-air entrainment event, one diurnal cycle of convective modulation. The commutation condition s⊥ts \perp t means environmental event ss is genuinely independent of the current storm history tt: a wind-shear pulse is independent of the storm’s prior SST exposure history (shear acts regardless of how warm the ocean was yesterday); a dry-air intrusion is independent of the prior eyewall structure (the dry-air acts on whatever convective organization exists). The one-step extension is,t:t→s⋆ti_{s,t} : t \to s \star t is the history extended by one environmental event; the restriction map H(is,t):Ts⋆t→TtH(i_{s,t}) : \mathcal{T}_{s \star t} \to \mathcal{T}_t sends a post-event storm state back to its pre-event state.

Weather

Formal definition

A Weather is a triple (W,t,ℓ)(W, t, \ell):

(W∈Wt,  t∈T,  ℓ∈Locale)(W \in W_t,\; t \in T,\; \ell \in \mathrm{Locale})

where:

  • WtW_t is the atmospheric state fiber at history tt — the complete Heyting algebra of atmospheric propositions at tt; elements of WtW_t are statements about temperature, pressure, humidity, and wind over locale ℓ\ell; the fiber inherits its Heyting algebra structure from the relational history fiber HtH_t via the locale embedding ℓ:Wt↪Ht\ell : W_t \hookrightarrow H_t
  • t∈Tt \in T is the analysis history — the position in the history monoid at which the atmospheric state is evaluated; each step s∈Σs \in \Sigma corresponds to one forward timestep of the atmospheric dynamical system, advancing the history from tt to t′=t⋆gen(s)t' = t \star \mathrm{gen}(s)
  • ℓ\ell is the locale — the spatial domain over which the atmospheric state WW is sampled; WtW_t is the presheaf fiber over ℓ\ell at tt; different locales (weather station, synoptic analysis grid, global model domain) yield sub-fibers related by locale restriction maps

The atmospheric nuclear pair. The fiber WtW_t carries two canonical nuclei, inherited from HtH_t and given meteorological content:
